output_text:
**Summary:** Critique of the four-paragraph intro for CausalAmbig, flagging a misattributed citation, an under-defined estimand, and several overclaims.

## Substantive issues

1. **Misattributed citation.** "modern double-machine-learning paradigm with cross-fitted nuisance regressions (Robins and Rotnitzky, 1995)" — Robins & Rotnitzky 1995 is about semiparametric efficiency / doubly-robust estimation, not DML or cross-fitting. The cross-fitted DML construction is Chernozhukov et al. 2018 ("Double/debiased machine learning for treatment and structural parameters"). Either swap the citation or, if you want both, cite Chernozhukov et al. (2018) for cross-fitting + Robins & Rotnitzky (1995) for the doubly-robust score.

2. **The target estimand is never defined.** You promise "nominal coverage of the ambiguity-aware estimand" but never say what that estimand is. Is it the set $\{\tau_v\}_{v \in V}$? The convex hull? An identified set under partial identification? The reader cannot evaluate the coverage claim without this. Add one sentence defining the inferential target before claiming coverage for it.

3. **"Union of pointwise CIs" is not 'calibrated' — it is conservative.** Taking a union of marginal CIs gives over-coverage of the set $\{\tau_v\}$, typically strictly above nominal unless you do a multiplicity correction (Bonferroni / max-t / bootstrap-quantile). The words "calibrated" and "coverage-controlled" oversell this. Either (a) describe it accurately as conservative, (b) show the multiplicity adjustment you actually use, or (c) report empirical coverage on the synthetic benchmark and let the numbers speak.

4. **"First framework" — likely overclaim.** The treatment-version / compound-treatment / treatment-variation-irrelevance literature (VanderWeele 2009; Hernán 2016 "Does water kill?"; Hernán & VanderWeele 2011) is directly relevant and not cited. Soften to "first end-to-end procedure" (which you already say in paragraph 4) and drop the "first framework" claim in paragraph 1, or position relative to that literature.

5. **"Shared identification assumption" is misleading.** Each treatment version $A_v$ has its own ignorability condition $Y(a_v) \perp A_v \mid X_v$, with a version-specific adjustment set. They are not literally shared — they are structurally analogous. Rephrase as "an analogous identification assumption for each version" or similar.

6. **Conceptual conflation in the treatment-version definition.** You define a version as "a binary indicator and a covariate-adjustment set." But the adjustment set is part of the identification strategy, not the treatment itself; two analysts could agree on the treatment indicator and disagree on the adjustment set, or vice versa. Either separate these two axes of ambiguity or justify bundling them.

7. **LaLonde is a poor showcase.** LaLonde's treatment (NSW job-training program participation) is unusually well pre-specified — that's why it's a benchmark. Featuring it as the headline application of a method whose pitch is ambiguous treatments undercuts the motivation. Either (a) describe the four "plausible treatment definitions" briefly so the reader can judge whether the ambiguity is real, or (b) lead with a case where ambiguity is unavoidable (the remote-work or statin examples you opened with).

8. **The synthetic benchmark result is near-tautological as stated.** "We constructed disagreement and observed set width grows with disagreement" tells the reader little. Reframe: report coverage at varying disagreement levels (the actual claim being tested), or report width vs. an oracle ambiguity set.

9. **The elicitation module is the most novel-sounding component and gets one sentence.** "Parses the query into a set of candidate treatment-version specifications" is doing a lot of work. If this is an LLM-based step, say so in the intro — it changes how the reader reads the contribution. If it is a hand-curated DSL, say that. As written, it reads like hand-waving.

10. **"Deployment-ready tool" claim is unsupported by the evaluation.** One real dataset (LaLonde) and one synthetic benchmark constructed by the authors is not deployment evidence. Either soften ("we provide a reference implementation") or add deployment evidence (case study with a domain collaborator, ablation on a held-out applied dataset).

## Smaller wording fixes

- "modern double-machine-learning paradigm" — drop "modern," it is redundant with DML and dates the paper.
- "modern doubly-robust outcome-regression / propensity-score combination" in paragraph 4 — same.
- Paragraph 4 mostly restates paragraph 1. Consider cutting it to one sentence on the contribution and using the saved space to define the estimand (issue 2).
- "Across all four versions, the ambiguity set excludes zero" — the ambiguity set is one object; what you mean is that each version-specific CI excludes zero, hence the union does. Rephrase.

## Uncertainty

- I have not verified the exact wording of Robins & Rotnitzky (1995) vs. (1994) — there are two relevant papers in that window; double-check the year you intend. The misattribution to *that pair of authors for cross-fitting* stands regardless.
- The treatment-version literature citations (VanderWeele 2009; Hernán 2016; Hernán & VanderWeele 2011) are from memory; verify exact titles/years before citing.
- Whether your "union of CIs" procedure is actually conservative vs. calibrated depends on details not in the paragraph — if you do apply a Bonferroni or bootstrap-max correction, ignore issue 3 and just say so in the intro.
